# ACF File Format

ACF is PolyConf's custom, human-readable configuration format. It is designed
to be small, easy to type, and easy to map into PolyConf's normalized tree:
named nodes, scalar values, repeated nodes, and comments.

This document describes the format as implemented today.

## Overview

An ACF file is made of:

- section headers
- scalar entries
- comments
- blank lines

Example:

```acf
# Generated by PolyConf
# Account settings
.account # user account
name = "colin stewart" # display name
developer = true

.account.info
active = true
```

This loads into paths such as:

```text
account.name
account.developer
account.info.active
```

## Whitespace

Leading and trailing whitespace around section headers, keys, values, and
comments is ignored.

These are equivalent:

```acf
name = colin
name=colin
  name   =   colin
```

Blank lines are allowed. Save output is normalized, so original blank-line
placement is not guaranteed to be preserved exactly.

## Comments

Comments begin with `#`.

```acf
# This is a full-line comment
name = colin # This is an inline comment
```

Comment text is stored without the leading `#`. For example, `# hello` is
stored internally as `hello`.

The parser ignores `#` inside quoted strings:

```acf
title = "dev # one"
```

PolyConf preserves comments loaded from a file and writes them back out when
saving. This is also used for converting comments between supported output
formats.

## Sections

A section header starts with a dot:

```acf
.account
```

Entries after a section header belong to that section until another section
header is read.

```acf
.account
name = colin
developer = true
```

This creates:

```text
account.name
account.developer
```

## Nested Sections

Nested sections use dotted paths:

```acf
.account.info
active = true
```

This creates:

```text
account.info.active
```

When opening a nested section, PolyConf attaches it to the latest existing
parent section with that name.

```acf
.account
name = first

.account
name = second

.account.info
active = true
```

In this example, `.account.info` attaches to the second `account`, so the value
is available at:

```text
account[1].info.active
```

## Repeated Sections

Opening the same section more than once creates repeated nodes.

```acf
.account
name = colin

.account
name = orim
```

This creates two `account` nodes:

```text
account.name
account[0].name
account[1].name
```

When an index is omitted, PolyConf uses index `0`.

## Scalar Entries

Scalar entries use `key = value`:

```acf
name = colin
id = 1
enabled = true
```

Values are stored as strings. Typed accessors convert them on read:

```cpp
config.get_string("account.name", "");
config.get_int("account.id", 0);
config.get_bool("account.enabled", false);
```

Invalid conversions return the caller-provided default value.

## Repeated Keys

Repeating a key in the same section creates repeated child nodes:

```acf
.server
addr = localhost
addr = 127.0.0.1
```

These can be read with indexed paths:

```text
server.addr
server.addr[0]
server.addr[1]
```

## Quoted Values

Values may be wrapped in double quotes:

```acf
name = "colin stewart"
title = "dev # one"
empty = ""
```

Quotes are useful when a value contains spaces, tabs, `#`, or `"`.

When loading, PolyConf removes one pair of surrounding double quotes when both
the first and last character of the value are `"`.

When saving, PolyConf quotes values that need quotes and escapes embedded
double quotes with a backslash.

## Booleans

Boolean values are stored as text and interpreted by `get_bool`.

Accepted true values:

```text
true
1
yes
on
```

Accepted false values:

```text
false
0
no
off
```

Boolean conversion is case-insensitive.

## Paths

PolyConf paths are dot-separated and may include indexes:

```text
account.name
account[1].name
account.info.active
server.addr[1]
```

Path indexes are zero-based.

## Current Limitations

The current ACF implementation is intentionally small. These details may become
stricter as the format matures:

- section and key names are lightly validated
- exact original whitespace is not preserved
- exact original blank-line placement is not preserved
- quoted values only remove surrounding quotes on load
- general escape-sequence decoding is not yet implemented

The recommended portable style is:

```acf
# Comment
.section
key = value
quoted = "value with spaces"
```
